Press enter or click to view image in full size

4 min read
Just now

--

How a deterministic code analysis tool turns its own limits into a feature — by refusing to guess where it can’t see.
A few days ago I wrote about seven real engineering gaps every codebase has — the blind spots most static analysis tools quietly miss.
Several people asked the obvious follow-up: how did you actually resolve them?
Here’s the honest answer. I didn’t.
Every code analysis tool eventually reaches the same boundary. Some questions about a program simply cannot be answered by reading the source code alone.
This isn’t a limitation of any particular tool — it’s a mathematical fact computer science has known for decades, formalized in Rice’s theorem: no static analysis can decide arbitrary semantic properties of a program.
A call whose target is chosen at runtime is undecidable statically. Full stop.
The challenge, then, isn’t pretending those limits don’t exist. It’s being honest about exactly where they are.
That’s the philosophy behind CodeTruth.
When people ask whether it solves the gaps that static analysis struggles with, my answer is simple: no. It makes those gaps visible, explains why they exist, and refuses to guess beyond the available evidence.
That single decision changes how the entire system is built.
Deterministic by design:
There is no AI model anywhere in the repository analysis path.
Every result comes from deterministic analysis: parsing the source code into a syntax tree, building the repository graph, tracing relationships between functions and classes, comparing what a project declares about itself against what the code implements, and validating every piece of evidence before it appears in a report.
Run the analysis twice on the same repository, and you get the same result. No probabilities. No hallucinations. No hidden reasoning you can’t inspect.
This matters more than it sounds. A tool that produces a different answer each time — or that can’t explain why it reached a conclusion — isn’t something you can build engineering decisions on. Determinism is the foundation everything else stands on.
It separates verified facts from known unknowns:
Consider dead code.
A function with no visible caller looks unused. But a web framework might invoke it through a route decorator. A plugin system might register it dynamically. A test runner might reach it through a fixture. None of these callers appear in the static call graph, because they happen through mechanisms the graph doesn’t capture.
Most tools flag the function as dead. That’s a dangerous guess.
CodeTruth does something different. It reads the decorators and base classes directly, cross-references them against the call graph, and separates the possibilities:

• Truly unreferenced code

• Framework-managed entry points

• Runtime-dispatched behavior that cannot be verified statically
The difference matters. One category is a cleanup candidate. The other could be a production outage waiting to happen. A tool that collapses them into a single “dead code” list is handing you a loaded gun.
Unknown doesn’t mean ignored:
Some calls are determined only at runtime. Some inheritance relationships extend into libraries outside the repository, where the parent class isn’t available to inspect.
Static analysis cannot fully resolve these cases. That’s not a bug — it’s the mathematical boundary I opened with.
The question is what a tool does when it hits that wall. Most tools quietly drop the unresolvable cases, and the gap disappears silently inside the report. You never learn it was there.
CodeTruth records them as known unknowns. It counts them exactly, names the reason each one couldn’t be resolved, and puts them in the report as a first-class result. The boundary becomes part of the output instead of vanishing inside the machine — so an engineer knows precisely where the verified floor ends and their own judgment needs to begin.
The tool questions itself:
Before deep analysis begins, CodeTruth evaluates how confident it is about its understanding of the repository — what kind of project this is, what framework it uses, how it’s structured.
During validation, I found something worth sitting with: higher confidence did not reliably mean higher accuracy. On the classification layer, the most confident labels were sometimes the least reliable.
I had a choice — hide that result, or surface it.
CodeTruth surfaces it. When its confidence is low, or even when it’s high but historically unreliable, it says so, and it lets the engineer decide whether additional review is needed. The tool treats its own conclusions as something that should also be verified, not trusted blindly.
A tool that doubts itself out loud is a strange thing to build. But it’s the honest thing to build.
Engineering is knowing where to stop:
The hardest engineering problem in this space isn’t pretending to solve the unsolvable. Anyone can print a confident answer.
The hard part is building a system disciplined enough to say:
“I can’t verify this with the available evidence.”
— and to provide a traceable reason, every single time.
That discipline is worth far more than a tool that always sounds sure of itself. Because confidence without evidence isn’t reliability. Transparency is.
The tool you can trust isn’t the one that claims to see everything. It’s the one that draws its own boundaries clearly — so you know exactly where to look yourself.
AI imagines. CodeTruth checks. Nature tests. Humans decide.
Question for engineers: Does your current tooling tell you where its evidence ends — or does it simply stop talking?
CodeTruth is open source: Zeeshan78699/CodeTruthAgent
